title: New machine-learning tool improves accuracy of genomics research
excerpt: University of Virginia School of Medicine scientists have identified a widespread source of error in a popular method for studying the genome and created a machine-learning tool to correct it. The free tool could improve the reliability of both conventional and single-cell data generated using this method, giving researchers a clearer view of how gene activity is controlled in health and disease and providing stronger foundations for future diagnostic and drug-development efforts.
content_html:
University of Virginia School of Medicine scientists have identified a widespread source of error in a popular method for studying the genome and created a machine-learning tool to correct it. The free tool could improve the reliability of both conventional and single-cell data generated using this method, giving researchers a clearer view of how gene activity is controlled in health and disease and providing stronger foundations for future diagnostic and drug-development efforts.

Although nearly every cell in the body contains the same DNA sequence, different cells use different sets of genes to maintain their identities and functions. Much of that control comes from the "epigenome"—chemical modifications and structural features of chromosomes that influence whether genes are turned on or off without changing the DNA sequence itself.

The CUT&Tag (Cleavage Under Targets & Tagmentation) method can map these epigenomic features efficiently from very small samples and even from individual cells. However, the UVA researchers, led by Chongzhi Zang, Ph.D., found a "hidden bias" in the method that creates artifacts resembling genuine biological signals.

"The DNA sequence is like the sheet music. The epigenome determines which notes to play, when they are played and by what instruments," said Zang, of UVA's Department of Genome Sciences and director of computational genomics at UVA Comprehensive Cancer Center.

"When a technical artifact looks like a real signal, researchers can be led toward the wrong biological mechanism. That risk is especially serious in single-cell data, where the true signals are already sparse."

Finding and fixing hidden bias

CUT&Tag has many advantages over prior methods and has quickly become popular among scientists. But it also has weaknesses that have gone unnoticed until recently. The assay relies on an enzyme called Tn5 transposase, which naturally favors open, accessible regions of the genome.

Zang and colleagues found that this preference can create misleading and confusing research results that the scientists described as "severe" after examining nearly 300 published datasets.

Teaching software to spot artifacts

Having discovered the unexpected scope of the problem, Zang and his team developed PATTY (Propensity Analyzer for Tn5 Transposase Yielded bias), a tool that uses machine learning to correct the CUT&Tag bias. It is effective both in conventional data from many cells and in sparser single-cell data, the scientists report. The research is published in the journal Nature Communications.

"Detecting true signals in noisy data is like finding a needle in a haystack, and it is even more difficult when many pieces of hay look like real needles," Zang said. "PATTY does not detect signals simply by subtracting a background. It learns how a real needle differs from hay and uses the learned model to reduce the artifact while preserving real signals."

Making cleaner data routine

Zang and his colleagues have made PATTY available as a free, open-source software package at GitHub and Zenodo. They hope their tool will improve genomic research and eventually help get new diagnostics and medicines to patients faster, a major mission of UVA's new Paul and Diane Manning Institute of Biotechnology.

"We believe that bias correction should become a routine part of CUT&Tag analysis," Zang said. "Cleaner data can keep scientists from wasting time and resources pursuing technical artifacts and can make real biological differences easier to see. More broadly, PATTY provides a conceptual framework for correcting similar biases in other genomic technologies."

The UVA research team consisted of Shengen Shawn Hu, Qingying Chen, Megan C. Grieco, Mengxue Tian and Zang.

Collaborators Zhangli Su and Anindya Dutta of the University of Alabama at Birmingham and Lin Liu of Shanghai Jiao Tong University also contributed. The scientists have no financial interest in the work.

Publication details

Shengen Shawn Hu et al, PATTY corrects open-chromatin bias for improved bulk and single-cell CUT&Tag profiling, Nature Communications (2026). DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-73599-8 On bioRxiv DOI: 10.1101/2025.09.02.673784

Who's behind this story?

Sadie Harley

BSc Life Sciences & Ecology. Microbiology lab background with pharmaceutical news experience in oil, gas, and renewable industries. Full profile →

Andrew Zinin

Master's in physics with research experience. Long-time science news enthusiast. Plays key role in Science X's editorial success. Full profile →

Citation: New machine-learning tool improves accuracy of genomics research (2026, July 20) retrieved 21 July 2026 from https://phys.org/news/2026-07-machine-tool-accuracy-genomics.html

sentences.en: The research is published in the journal Nature Communications.
sentences.zh: 该研究发表在《自然通讯》杂志上。

sentences.en: "PATTY does not detect signals simply by subtracting a background.
sentences.zh: “PATTY 不能简单地通过减去背景来检测信号。

sentences.en: It learns how a real needle differs from hay and uses the learned model to reduce the artifact while preserving real signals."
sentences.zh: 它了解真实的针与干草的区别，并使用学习的模型来减少伪影，同时保留真实信号。”

sentences.zh: “更清晰的数据可以让科学家们避免浪费时间和资源来追求技术工件，并且可以使真正的生物学差异更容易看到。

sentences.en: More broadly, PATTY provides a conceptual framework for correcting similar biases in other genomic technologies."
sentences.zh: 更广泛地说，PATTY 提供了一个概念框架，用于纠正其他基因组技术中的类似偏差。”

content_html_zh:
弗吉尼亚大学医学院的科学家们发现了一种流行的基因组研究方法中存在广泛的错误来源，并创建了一种机器学习工具来纠正它。 该免费工具可以提高使用这种方法生成的传统数据和单细胞数据的可靠性，使研究人员能够更清楚地了解健康和疾病中基因活性的控制方式，并为未来的诊断和药物开发工作提供更坚实的基础。

尽管体内几乎每个细胞都含有相同的 DNA 序列，但不同的细胞使用不同的基因组来维持其身份和功能。 这种控制大部分来自“表观基因组”——染色体的化学修饰和结构特征，它们影响基因的开启或关闭，而不改变 DNA 序列本身。

CUT&Tag（目标下切割和标记）方法可以从非常小的样本甚至单个细胞中有效地映射这些表观基因组特征。 然而，由 Chongzhi Zang 博士领导的 UVA 研究人员发现，这种方法存在“隐藏的偏差”，这种方法会产生类似于真实生物信号的伪影。

“DNA 序列就像乐谱。 表观基因组决定了要演奏哪些音符、何时演奏以及用什么乐器演奏，”UVA 基因组科学系和 UVA 综合癌症中心计算基因组学主任 Zang 说。

“当技术制品看起来像真实信号时，研究人员可能会被引导到错误的生物机制上。 这种风险在单细胞数据中尤其严重，因为真实信号已经稀疏。”

发现并纠正隐藏的偏见

CUT&Tag 与以前的方法相比具有许多优点，并迅速受到科学家的欢迎。 但它也有一些直到最近才被注意到的弱点。 该检测依赖于一种名为 Tn5 转座酶的酶，该酶自然有利于基因组中开放、可接近的区域。

Zang 及其同事发现，这种偏好可能会产生误导性和令人困惑的研究结果，科学家在检查了近 300 个已发表的数据集后将其描述为“严重”。

教学软件如何发现伪影

在发现问题的意外范围后，Zang 和他的团队开发了 PATTY（Tn5 转座酶产生偏差的倾向分析器），这是一种使用机器学习来纠正 CUT&Tag 偏差的工具。 科学家报告说，它对于来自许多细胞的传统数据和稀疏的单细胞数据都有效。 该研究发表在《自然通讯》杂志上。

“在嘈杂的数据中检测真实信号就像大海捞针一样，当许多干草看起来像真正的针时，这就更加困难，”臧说。 “PATTY 不能简单地通过减去背景来检测信号。 它了解真实的针与干草的区别，并使用学习的模型来减少伪影，同时保留真实信号。”

使数据更干净成为常态

Zang 和他的同事在 GitHub 和 Zenodo 上将 PATTY 作为免费开源软件包提供。 他们希望他们的工具能够改善基因组研究，并最终帮助更快地为患者提供新的诊断方法和药物，这是弗吉尼亚大学新成立的保罗和黛安曼宁生物技术研究所的一项主要使命。

“我们相信偏差校正应该成为 CUT&Tag 分析的常规部分，”Zang 说。 “更清晰的数据可以让科学家们避免浪费时间和资源来追求技术工件，并且可以使真正的生物学差异更容易看到。 更广泛地说，PATTY 提供了一个概念框架，用于纠正其他基因组技术中的类似偏差。”

UVA研究团队由Shengen Shawn Hu、Qingying Chen、Megan C. Grieco, Mengxue Tian and Zang.

阿拉巴马大学伯明翰分校的合作者Zhangli Su和Anindya Dutta以及上海交通大学的Lin Liu也做出了贡献。 科学家们对这项工作没有经济利益。
